speaker: interviewer
text: It's December 26th, 1996. I'm interviewing Mr. Yehudah Bakon. My name is Yael Ravon. The interview is taking place in Jerusalem, Israel. And the language will be English. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Today's the 26 of December 1996. I am Yael Ravon-- Y-A-E-L R-A-V-O-N. I have the privilege of interviewing Mr. Yehudah Bakon-- Y-E-H-U-D-A B-A-C-O-N. The interview is taking place in Jerusalem, Israel. The interview will be mostly in English, and maybe partly in Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number one. What's your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yehudah Bakon.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Mahrisch-Ostrau, today Ostrava, in Czechoslovakia, Moravia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us a little bit about the town you were born in? How many Jews were there? How was the town altogether?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a town at the border of Silesia, Germany, Poland, and Moravia. And I was born to a family of Jews who came, immigrated in the First World War from Poland to Czechoslovakia. And there was a small population of Jews, about, I would say, 7,000 Jews. I remember when the transport went to Theresienstadt, there were only four transports, which means around 3 and 1/2, 4,000 Jews went from Ostrava to Theresienstadt. But many, of course, immigrated before.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the town itself, was it a beautiful town? Was it an old town?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was an industrial center, coal mines and steel factories.

speaker: interviewer
text: When we were you born, the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 28 of July, '29.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, so how old are you now?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's the name of your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was Yisra'el, and my mother was [? Ethel-- ?] Ester.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And did they have any other names? Did you father have a second name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they called him, [? Sroel, ?] Yisra'el. And my mother was called [? Ethel. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. And did you have any brothers, sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had two sisters, Hani and [? Rela ?], today Rifche. Rifche was lucky and could immigrate in '39, shortly after the German occupied Czechoslovakia. And Hani--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did she immigrate to?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Israel. And I was left with my sister and parents, and later on came to Theresienstadt and to Auschwitz with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us something about the general atmosphere in your home, whether your parents were religious? Did you go to the-- did you keep Shabbat? How was the atmosphere in that respect?

speaker: interviewee
text: The atmosphere was of a small, bourgeois, you would call it today lower-middle class. We had a tiny little factory with the brother of my father for leather goods. And we were, you would say today, Masortim. I went, Saturday, to the synagogue, not all the time, but to all high festivals.

speaker: interviewer
text: Also on Saturdays, on Shabbat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not always. But we went to a Jewish school. It was like a normal school, but mainly the difference was they were for Jewish children. And we had modern Hebrew. And the language was Czech and German, depended on who just occupied Mahrisch-Ostrau. In the time when it belonged to Czechoslovakia, the main language was Czech, and the secondary language was German. And later on German was the main subject and Czech was the second language. But of course, we also studied modern Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: You are speaking about the time of the German occupation when German was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, in the beginning--

speaker: interviewer
text: --you went to school then? We should speak about that time. But we are speaking now before the war, before things really went bad. So it was actually school which was Jewish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Jewish school.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a Zionistic school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a Zionistic school, but there--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you had Hebrew from very early age?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. And we--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you spoke Hebrew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I thought they knew Hebrew. But when I came to Israel, I hardly could speak.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you could read?

speaker: interviewee
text: I could read. And I could understand a bit of Hebrew, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: So does that mean that your parents were Zionists?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents were Zionists, because my uncle was even the president of the Mizrachi movement. And his--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Ostrava?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Ostrava. And his wife was the president of the women organization, something like [NON-ENGLISH] you would call it today. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was you uncle? But your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father wasn't an active Zionist, but he was a Zionist, of course. And we had the blue box a little Keren Kayemeth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Keren Kayemeth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever consider immigrating before the war, immigrating to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had knowledge about Palestine, as I remember very well the first Palestine, or film about Palestine in about '35. And we went even to a kind of Jewish organization, like [NON-ENGLISH]. These were the two organizations. I was too young to join them, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, but I'm asking something very specific. Did your parents consider ever immigrating, leaving Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. They played with this idea. But of course, I was a small child. And I only know it-- that it was very urgent when things became not so normal, I mean after '34.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. But still, your sister Rifche, she had immigrated to Palestine before--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. No, no, no, under the Germans. Under the German occupation.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning it was still possible.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did she manage?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was in the age of [NON-ENGLISH], or [NON-ENGLISH]. And she was about, I think, 10 or 11 years or something like that. And she came to Israel. She was-- was she oriented Zionistically? She was-- Through my parents, yes. We were oriented in Zionism.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she came here to family or to the kibbutz?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She came just [NON-ENGLISH]. She was in a kind of a moshav or kibbutz. She was a member of a kibbutz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she's still now a member of a kibbutz?

speaker: interviewee
text: She's now of a moshav?

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Masu'ot Yitzhak.

speaker: interviewer
text: Masu'ot Yitzhak. So she-- so fortunately someone from your family survived this way. Now, did you have-- do you remember your grandfathers, grandmothers, uncles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I remember very well my grandfather and grandmother from the mother's side, because they were living in the same building as we. And I remember also my grandmother from the mother's side. My grandfather from the mother's side died before I was born. And I, of course, care his name, Yehudah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. And did you have any--

speaker: interviewee
text: Uncles?

speaker: interviewer
text: Uncles and cousins and children?

speaker: interviewee
text: One uncles was staying in the same-- from the mother's side, in the same time in Mahrisch-Ostrau. And the brother of my father was also staying in Mahrisch-Ostrau. And they shared the same fabrik together.

speaker: interviewer
text: The factory. So actually, you were in contact with your cousins?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, [INAUDIBLE] was just around the corner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Also they had children and you came together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. We were in the same school later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were good connections in the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you did you have any contact with non-Jewish children in Ostrava?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only with the children we played on the courtyard of our house. And these were interrupted, of course, in '39 when the German occupied Mahrisch-Ostrau.

speaker: interviewer
text: But before the German occupation, did you feel that you were Jewish? Were there any antisemitic sign-- signs in the streets?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not before the occupation. Only the antisemitism started, at least in Mahrisch-Ostrau, around '38, when we heard about what's happening in Austria, the so-called Anschluss. Then there was a fascistic Czech movement. And we saw the first kind of posters and signs on the street-- Jews out, or Jews go to Palestine, things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. But in the street, did you feel any antisemitism between the children? Did they ever offend you? Did they ever say anything to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it only started in '39. And somehow, I was not so badly hurt as the other one, because my face, at that time, wasn't so Jewish-looking.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were a little blond kid with blue eyes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, light eyes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. So did you ever leave Ostrava before that war started, because of the Germans? Did you run away from the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember when there was the thing-- the thing with the Anschluss-- the occupation of Sudeten, that we run away. But it was only for a couple of days. And then we came back. But we only run away to another place to Slovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to Slovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did it happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was around '38.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. But then you came back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we came back, because the mobilization, at the time they called it. There was a mobilization. And nobody knew what will happen. But after the German occupied the [NON-ENGLISH], what was called then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Area, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Area of the Sudetenland. Things calmed down till '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Your family were afraid that they will-- the Germans are going to occupy your town, so you ran away.

speaker: interviewee
text: And therefore, we ran away. But we came after--

speaker: interviewer
text: You came back.

speaker: interviewee
text: --a couple, maybe even two or three days, we came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you stay during these days?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember we stood with somebody who was taking a kind of job in our factory. And some officer in-- how you would call it today? A merchant who worked for us in Slovakia. And he gave us his flat. That's what I have in the memory.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. So at least you had where to go. And then you came back. And things were normal.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not so much, because we heard all kinds of rumors, mainly from Poland and also from people--

speaker: interviewer
text: We are speaking about '38?

speaker: interviewee
text: '38. I think the rumors we heard were people who ran away from Austria. And one of the first memories are that my father came once back, he came usually for lunch. And he told us what a strange thing happened to me. Some men came to the factory and asked for help, because Mahrisch-Ostrau was, as I told you, on a border. And they run away through Mahrisch-Ostrau to Poland, which was, at that time, a way to run away to other countries.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he asked for some financial help. Most probably, the Jewish community sent him to me. And I gave him the money or whatever he wanted. But how strange. Why did he have to tell me this story that he was a banker, and now he is penniless? Anyhow, I gave him the money, which meant that this story that he was a banker, some-- at that time, somebody very rich and so on-- And he had to run away. When did-- where was this man from? From Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Vienna. That was, even in his eye, unbelievable. We couldn't imagine things like that, that this kind of brutality could happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a Jew, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father helped him?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father helped him. And that was the story which was--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you remember your father telling it at home to you, to the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, around the table at lunch time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was very--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was shocking.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was shocking.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that he couldn't understand it, why he had to say it after he got the money.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was a refugee from Vienna who came over. Yes. Well, did you see any-- andy refugee children in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: We saw a few children, which actually came-- I don't know if it was '38, maybe it was even a little later-- to our class. Because, as I told you, Mahrisch-Ostrau was a place where people passed for one reason or another, to escape. And the very first time into our class came German children, Austrian children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish children, because we were a Jewish school. And it was strange in our eyes. Suddenly different children with a different kind of mentality, and of course, language. They didn't know so well Czech. And that is, well, I remember it very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you absorbed them?

speaker: interviewee
text: We absorbed them as much as we could. And I remember even that these children, later on, were helped. They got free meals. Every day they went to a different family and got a meal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cold lunch. They were with their parents, of course?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were usually only with their mothers, for one reason or another. I can't remember why. But somehow they didn't have any more of their fathers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do remember any of their experiences? Did they tell you what happened to them? Why did they have to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I only knew that they had to run away. And that was the beginning, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now when did the German conquer your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is--

speaker: interviewer
text: Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: --quite in my memory, because it was the 14, I think, of March, '39. And suddenly, we saw the first thing was that I saw trains and trains and trains loaded with soldiers. And they were very huge in my eyes. Later on, I knew that they sent only the high, I mean, a meter, 80 or so--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, really?

speaker: interviewee
text: --soldiers to make an impression. And Mahrisch-Ostrau was even one day before Prague occupied. Prague was a day later occupied.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I remember there were many refugees from Slovakia and other places. Even one uncle came and was very nervous, and said the German are occupying Czechoslovakia. And there was a great tension, and he was running away to another place. But for us children, it was interesting what's happening We were living could not so far away from the railway station that was looking there. And then we went out in the street. And there were some tanks and slogans, Recht gehen links ubertrat, which means, Sonny, go right and cross on the left side. What did it meant? In Czechoslovakia, it was different, like today, still in England, everything was on the left. Oh, so they changed it.

speaker: interviewee
text: One of the first things was right and left. The second thing was, as a child, we run to the post office, and we wanted to get the first issue of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Stamps.

speaker: interviewee
text: The new stamps. And the stamp, I got it. And that was 'die Stadt der schwarzen Diamanten dank den Fuhrer fur ihre befreiung.' Which meant the city, or the town, of the black diamonds, coal, thanks the Fuhrer for their liberation. And then we went to the streets and saw tanks, and we touched them. Actually, it was armored cars. Why? Because in the rumors, they said, oh, no, they are not really armored. The German don't have so much armors. They are from cartoon. And I wanted to prove it for myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Paper, that is it was--

speaker: interviewee
text: If it's a cartoon or paper or real armor.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a real one. And it was real.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was real armor. And that was the first impression. And slowly, we get to feel the hand of the occupation. I mean, from day to day, things went from bad to worse, more and more restriction. The antisemitism was on the high. There was more and more slogans. In Ostrava-- In Ostrava there were quite a large population of German, real German.

speaker: interviewer
text: Volksdeutsche.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were German. And they went, even before, with the white socks and there was a special movement. And next to us was the German theater, which I knew before, because my father liked opera, and sometimes I went with him to the next building, which was the German opera and theater house. And suddenly these German were more and more noisy and started with showing off all kinds of propaganda, windows with the newspaper, NSDAP, or however it was called. And we saw pictures of Sturmer. And as a child, I was curious, and I looked on this terribly caricatures against Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: They already put it on the-- where? On the wall?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the streets.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the streets.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you understood it already, that it's against the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I knew German. Of course I knew what's happening.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you in '39?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in '39, I was exactly 10 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: 10. And you knew German?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I knew German.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you understood--

speaker: interviewee
text: I could read it, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --the caricatures, where they put the Stumer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Even the German special--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sign.

speaker: interviewee
text: Gothic-- we even wrote in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. You could read in Gothic?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I could read it very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you knew exactly what's going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, we knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how was it-- how did this occupation affect the life of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were more and more restricted. And soon came all kinds of rumors about Poland, what's happening there. Soon we had to give away our-- a room in our flat. We had to rent it. And I remember that room we rented was a German moved in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you really rent it for money? Or is was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. At the beginning--

speaker: interviewer
text: It was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the beginning it was-- it wasn't taken. It was still, in a way, normal. We rent the room. One was a German woman, [? Florian ?] was her name. A very nice woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she worked for the German army? She was--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she was, in my eyes, an elderly woman. Maybe she was 35.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe she was 30.

speaker: interviewee
text: 35, 40. Because later on, her son came to visit us, a soldier. And I have a story about him. But in the beginning, everything was more or less normal, but we were forbidden slowly to go to parks-- Jews and dogs. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to the dogs?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] are forbidden.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to the dogs, the pets? You were not allowed to have pets?

speaker: interviewee
text: It came later. First of all, we were forbidden. The park was written to Jew and dogs, Eintritt verboten. It's forbidden to go in. We were not allowed to go to cinemas or to swimming pools and other places. Later on, there were rationalized the food. And the Jews could only buy much later and only in certain shops. And so when we came in the evening, we got only the leftovers. Still later, we didn't got the same rations like other people. No milk and so on. More and more restriction. Then, there was a law. We had to give away all our jewelries and pets and bicycles and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Also radio?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but at that time, no many people had radios. And more and more restriction. Then there was something very strange. Mahrisch-Ostrau was the very first city or town which evacuated, which there was a forced evacuation of Jews to Nisko. And this was in '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is Nisko?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nisko is in Poland. And they sent all the Jews, about from 18 to 45 or something like that, had to register and were sent to Nisko. And that was the beginning of deportation. It was one of the first--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened in Nisko?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the beginning of a kind of deportation of Jews. And Eichmann was the leader of this--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was in charge of this operation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Operation, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, was Nisko a concentration camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was built. There was nothing there. But the Jews from Mahrisch-Ostrau, they called it Umschulung. They want reeducate them or re-- give them other profession.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was still before the war started? It was still '38?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was '39, soon after the occupation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me, '39, soon after the occupation. And your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was something very curious. My father said, I don't need another occupation. I have a good occupation. I can do things from leather and so on. And he hit himself. I mean he didn't went to the call. And I remember--

speaker: interviewer
text: All the Jews were called to come?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, only men.

speaker: interviewer
text: Only men, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And only men who were not, maybe, high officials in the Jewish community, were not teachers. They were not in this. But they took all the men.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you father was in that category?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he was in that category, but just he didn't went to it. And it was, of course, very dangerous. And he called a Czech doctor just to have a kind of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Certificate.

speaker: interviewee
text: Certificate.

speaker: interviewer
text: That he's very sick.

speaker: interviewee
text: That he has a cold or so. But it was, of course, just a bluff. And after only a week or so, he slowly went on the street. Again, he showed himself, and we were very, very afraid, first of all, that somebody could denounce him. And we knew there are people, even some of the Jews, who were denouncing-- who were working with the Gestapo.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I remember even the story that somebody met him on the street and said, Mr. Bakon, you are alive? I heard that you died in Nisko. And there were all kinds of rumors, of course. Did any from-- anyone from your family go to Nisko, your uncles?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. From my-- because--

speaker: interviewer
text: I am speaking about your uncles.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only-- friends, of course, all my--

speaker: interviewer
text: Friends of your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My friends from the school, their fathers were in Nisko. And I remember the very first day when we came to school, our teacher gave us a talk and said, now, poor children, you are a half-- you have no father. And all of us were very sad. And I had to play that even my father is there.

speaker: interviewer
text: To pretend, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because, yeah, I had to pretend. And at that time, it wasn't so dangerous. And somehow nothing happened. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me. We shall now have a little stop.

speaker: interviewee
text: A rest, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is the end of the first tape. And we shall soon go on.
